coredata.dc:description: The development of novel electrode materials with admirable electrochemical behaviors is a new opportunity to meet the current demand for the high energy density of supercapacitors. Herein, a facile molecular precursor route is used to synthesize dual functionalized Fe2O3 two-dimensional nanosheets with phosphate and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (P-Fe2O3/NCDs) modifications. Taking full use of the excellent characteristics of each component, the optimized P-Fe2O3/NCDs anode material achieves superior electrochemical features with an outstanding specific capacitance of 453 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. The cycling stability of P-Fe2O3/NCDs nanosheets is as high as 91.0 % after 10,000 cycles at 15 A g−1. Subsequently, phosphate and NCDs modified Co9S8 (P-Co9S8/NCDs) nanoflowers with convenient ion transport properties are fabricated, which possess a satisfied electrochemical capacity of 569 C g−1 at 1 A g−1 with desirable cycling stability at 15 A g−1. Impressively, the designed P-Co9S8/NCDs//P-Fe2O3/NCDs hybrid supercapacitor can achieve an excellent energy density of 85.1 Wh kg−1 at 937.5 W kg−1 and can retain 93.9 % capacitance efficiency after 20,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. The superior properties of the dual functionalized electrode materials provide a new choice for constructing advanced hybrid supercapacitors.

head.ce:title: Dual functionalized Fe2O3 nanosheets and Co9S8 nanoflowers with phosphate and nitrogen-doped carbon dots for advanced hybrid supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of novel electrode materials with admirable electrochemical behaviors is a new opportunity to meet the current demand for the high energy density of supercapacitors. Herein, a facile molecular precursor route is used to synthesize dual functionalized Fe2O3 two-dimensional nanosheets with phosphate and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (P-Fe2O3/NCDs) modifications. Taking full use of the excellent characteristics of each component, the optimized P-Fe2O3/NCDs anode material achieves superior electrochemical features with an outstanding specific capacitance of 453 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. The cycling stability of P-Fe2O3/NCDs nanosheets is as high as 91.0 % after 10,000 cycles at 15 A g−1. Subsequently, phosphate and NCDs modified Co9S8 (P-Co9S8/NCDs) nanoflowers with convenient ion transport properties are fabricated, which possess a satisfied electrochemical capacity of 569 C g−1 at 1 A g−1 with desirable cycling stability at 15 A g−1. Impressively, the designed P-Co9S8/NCDs//P-Fe2O3/NCDs hybrid supercapacitor can achieve an excellent energy density of 85.1 Wh kg−1 at 937.5 W kg−1 and can retain 93.9 % capacitance efficiency after 20,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. The superior properties of the dual functionalized electrode materials provide a new choice for constructing advanced hybrid supercapacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, energy shortage has become a major problem and challenge for the sustainable development of human society. Developing an economical and efficient energy storage system is particularly urgent to meet the growing energy demand [1]. Supercapacitors have received considerable concern compared with secondary batteries because of their longer cycle life, faster charge–discharge behavior, and higher power density [2]. However, the limited energy density of supercapacitors severely restricted their practical applications [3,4]. How to optimize the energy density of supercapacitors without sacrificing their power density has become the research focus in the field of supercapacitors [5]. Recently, advanced hybrid supercapacitors with increased energy density considerably have been constructed by researchers. It is demonstrated that the successful fabricating of hybrid supercapacitors with splendid electrochemical features lies on the advances in electrode materials [6,7]. Accordingly, the design and construction of appropriate capacitive anodes and battery-type cathodes are crucial for hybrid supercapacitors.

The commonly used anode materials for hybrid supercapacitors are carbonaceous materials. Due to the double electric layer characteristics, these anodes always suffer from low capacitances, which hinder the widespread promotion of hybrid supercapacitors [8]. Nowadays, some pseudocapacitive transition metal compounds operating in wide negative potential windows have fascinated much attention [9–11]. Among them, iron-based materials with various valence states are considered promising candidates due to their merits, such as satisfactory theoretical capacitances, low cost, and environmentally friendly [12,13]. It is noteworthy that the rational design of nanostructured iron-based electrodes with high active surface areas can shorten the ion transport distance, thus effectively boosting the electrochemical properties [14]. Some desirable architectures such as nanoparticles and nanorods have been constructed in this regard. On the other hand, constructing hybrid supercapacitors with high-performance battery-like cathodes is another scalable avenue to optimize the energy density further. Cobalt-based compounds have been extremely examined as promising cathodes due to their rich redox reactions, superior capacities, and environmental amiability [15,16]. Compared with iron-based materials, cobalt-based compounds possess more tunable morphologies, such as nanoparticles, nanorods, nanoneedles, and microspheres, which contain sufficient spaces to store electrolyte ions [17]. Unfortunately, the electrochemical properties of these iron and cobalt-based materials are still restricted by the unsatisfied conductivity and inferior cycling ability, which severely impede their implementation as anodes and cathodes for hybrid supercapacitors [18].

Herein, we demonstrate a systematic strategy to functionalize the surface of Fe2O3 nanosheets and Co9S8 nanoflowers with phosphate ions and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). As a means of surface optimization, phosphate functionalization can effectively increase the electrical conductivity of materials [19]. Due to the low electronegativity of phosphorus, it can promote the rapid transfer of the 3d electrons of transition metals and thus stimulate fast faraday redox reactions [20,21]. Meanwhile, the monodisperse and zero-dimensional carbon dots own fascinating electrochemical characteristics, exhibiting great potential in energy storage [22]. The distinctive edge quantum effect, excellent surface modification ability, and ultra-small size of carbon dots can bring additional pathways for electron transport, resulting in significantly improved conductivity and cyclic stability [23]. Consequently, the resultant phosphate and NCDs dual functionalized Fe2O3 (P-Fe2O3/NCDs) nanosheets and Co9S8 (P-Co9S8/NCDs) nanoflowers display admirable electrochemical properties with a specific capacitance of 453 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a specific capacity of 569 C g−1 at 1 A g−1, respectively. More importantly, a hybrid supercapacitor constructed by P-Fe2O3/NCDs and P-Co9S8/NCDs electrodes achieves a remarkable energy density of 85.1 Wh kg−1 and ultra-high stability of 93.9 % capacitance retention over 20,000 cycles, which has potential application prospects in energy storage.

FeCl3·6H2O, (C2H5)2NCSSNa·3H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, NaH2PO4·H2O, KOH, thiourea, diethanolamine, and ethylene glycol were all supplied from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polytetrafluoroethylene was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade.

First, 25 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O and 75 mmol of (C2H5)2NCSSNa·3H2O were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water, respectively. After ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, the two solutions were mixed and stirred for 3 h, then allowed to stand for 24 h. The precipitates were filtered by suction, washed with deionized water, and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the P-Fe2O3/NCDs two-dimensional nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The NCDs powders were synthesized similarly to our previous work [24]. Representatively, NCDs powders were added to 35 mL of ethylene glycol and sonicated for 30 min. Then, 200 mg of Fe(DDTC)3 was dissolved in the above solution by sonication for another 30 min. The mixture was reacted at 180 °C for 12 h in a 50 mL autoclave. The precipitates were centrifuged and cleaned with water and ethanol. After drying at 60 °C for 12 h, the samples were calcinated at 400 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. The composites synthesized with 10, 15, and 20 mg of NCDs were marked as Fe2O3/NCDs-1, Fe2O3/NCDs-2, and Fe2O3/NCDs-3. Bare Fe2O3 was synthesized in the absence of NCDs. Finally, Fe2O3/NCDs-2 precursor (30 mg) and NaH2PO2·H2O (150, 300, and 450 mg) were put on two sides of a porcelain boat and calcined at 300 °C for 1 h at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 in N2 atmosphere. The composites with different phosphating mass ratios were named P-Fe2O3/NCDs-1, P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2, and P-Fe2O3/NCDs-3. As a comparison, phosphate functionalized Fe2O3 (P-Fe2O3) was also synthesized in the same way as P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the P-Co9S8/NCDs flower-like composites
ce:section.ce:para: Quintessentially, NCDs powders (0, 8, 10, and 12 mg) were added to 12 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. Subsequently, 1 mmol of CoCl2·6H2O and 4 mmol of thiourea were dissolved in the above solution. Another 12 mL of diethanolamine was injected and continuously stirred for 10 min. The solution was reacted at 180 °C for 4 h in a 30 mL autoclave. The precipitates were centrifuged, cleaned, and dried in an oven. The samples were labeled as bare Co9S8, Co9S8/NCDs-1, Co9S8/NCDs-2, and Co9S8/NCDs-3. For preparing P-Co9S8/NCDs composites, Co9S8/NCDs-2 (30 mg) and NaH2PO2·H2O (150, 300, and 450 mg) were placed in a porcelain boat and heated at 300 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere. The products were named P-Co9S8/NCDs-1, P-Co9S8/NCDs-2, and P-Co9S8/NCDs-3. Phosphate functionalized Co9S8 (P-Co9S8) was synthesized in the same way as P-Co9S8/NCDs-2.

The phase structures were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Smartlab 9kw). The valence distribution of the composites was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha + ). The specific surface area and pore size distribution were investigated on the micromeritics ASAP 2460 instrument. The morphological characteristics of the products were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200x). The composition of the composites was evaluated by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, attached to SEM). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and element mapping analysis were performed on FEI Talos F200x. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern was acquired from a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN TEM microscope.

The reference and auxiliary electrodes in the three-electrode configuration were Hg/HgO (1 M KOH) and platinum sheet. The active material, polytetrafluoroethylene, and acetylene black (8:1:1) were mixed in a mortar using ethanol as the solvent. The resultant slurry was glazed on nickel foam at 10 MPa and heated at 60 °C for 12 h. The materials loaded on nickel foam were about 2.0 mg cm−2. The specific capacitance (C 1) of the Fe2O3-based electrode and specific capacity (C 2) of the Co9S8-based electrode can be calculated by the following formulas [25]:
(1)
C 1
= I t /
m 1
V
(2)
C 2
= I t /
m 1
where m 1, V, t, and I are the mass of the active material, potential window, constant current discharge time, and current density, respectively. The synthesized P-Fe2O3/NCDs and P-Co9S8/NCDs acted as the negative and positive electrodes in the hybrid supercapacitor. The loading ratio of P-Fe2O3/NCDs and P-Co9S8/NCDs active substances was 1.25:1 to follow the charge conservation. The energy density (E) and power density (P) of P-Co9S8/NCDs//P-Fe2O3/NCDs were calculated based on the total weight of active substances (m 2) by the following formulas [26]:
(3)
E = I ∫
Vdt / (
m 2
)
(4)
P = E / t

The above tests were carried out on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation. The cycle properties of P-Fe2O3/NCDs, P-Co9S8/NCDs, and P-Co9S8/NCDs//P-Fe2O3/NCDs were investigated on the Neware CT-4008 testing instrument.

The P-Fe2O3/NCDs nanosheets are prepared by using Fe(DDTC)3 as the molecular precursor. The synthesis process is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . First, NCDs were uniformly deposited on the precursor by a simple solvothermal strategy, and then Fe2O3/NCDs nanosheets were formed by calcination in the air atmosphere. Finally, phosphate ions were attached to the surface of Fe2O3/NCDs by calcining and decomposing NaH2PO2 at a high temperature to form P-Fe2O3/NCDs. The P-Co9S8/NCDs flower-like hierarchical structure was also fabricated by the facile two-step strategy of solvothermal and subsequent phosphorization process.

XRD test was adopted to characterize the phase structure. As presented in Fig. 2 a, the characteristic peaks in Fe2O3/NCDs-2 nanosheets closely match the cubic phase of Fe2O3 (JCPDS 39–1346). The absence of characteristic peaks of NCDs can be attributed to the ultra-small size and/or low content of NCDs in the composites [27,28]. After the secondary phosphorization engineering, the diffraction peaks of P-Fe2O3/NCDs still correspond to the cubic phase of Fe2O3, confirming that the crystal phase is well-maintained after the phosphate modification. To further explore the distribution of valence states and elements in Fe2O3/NCDs and P-Fe2O3/NCDs, XPS analysis was performed. The overview XPS spectrum of Fe2O3/NCDs-2 verifies the coexistence of C, N, Fe, and O elements (Fig. S1). The survey spectrum of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 exhibits the additional P signal, confirming the successful phosphate functionalization of Fe2O3/NCDs. The C 1 s spectra of Fe2O3/NCDs-2 and P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 in Fig. 2b exhibit three distinct peaks at 284.7, 286.1, and 288.7 eV, attributing to the bonding of C–C/C = C, C-O/C = N, and O-C = O/C-N [29]. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2c) can be fitted into four characteristic peaks at 398.9, 400.0, 400.8, and 401.9 eV attributable to pyridine N, pyrrole N, graphite N, and oxidized N, respectively [30,31]. Generally, pyridine N and pyrrole N exist in the carbon skeleton can effectively improve the specific capacitance of carbonaceous material [32]. The Fe 2p spectra in Fig. 2d include two groups of spin–orbit peaks (2p3/2 and 2p1/2). The peaks centered at 710.9 and 724.5 eV belong to Fe2+ species, while the other peaks at 713.4 and 727.7 eV are indexed to the oxidized Fe3+ in Fe2O3 [33,34]. The binding energies at 719.0 and 733.5 eV correspond to the satellite (sat.) peaks of Fe 2p [35]. The O 1 s spectrum of Fe2O3/NCDs-2 in Fig. 2e exhibits the characteristics of Fe-O, C = O, C-O, and O–H groups centered at 530.2, 530.8, 531.8, and 532.9 eV, respectively. After phosphorization treatment, an intense peak at 531.1 eV in P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 can be attributed to H2PO4 -, while the shoulder peak that appeared at 532.3 eV corresponds to the PO3 - species [36]. Moreover, the strong peak at 133.8 eV in P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 (Fig. 2f) can be assigned to the P-O bond [37]. These results verify that the phosphate ions were generated during the thermal decomposition of NaH2PO2.

SEM and TEM characterizations were performed to conduct in-depth research on the morphologies of the samples. It can be found that the bare Fe2O3 in Fig. 3 a synthesized by using Fe(DDTC)3 as the molecular precursor presents a representative sheet-like structure. After adding NCDs, the negative charge carried by NCDs has a powerful electrostatic effect with Fe3+, which induces the growth of small-size nanosheets (Fig. 3b). As exhibited in Fig. 3c, P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 still maintains the sheet-like architecture after the secondary phosphorization treatment. These nanosheets are assembled by a large number of nanoparticles (Fig. 3d). The lamellar morphology of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 can be obviously observed from the TEM diagram in Fig. 3e. The lattice fringes of 0.21 and 0.25 nm in Fig. 3f well match the (100) and (311) crystal planes of NCDs and Fe2O3. The SAED pattern of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 is additionally attached to Fig. 3f. The concentric rings can be assigned to the (311) and (440) lattice planes of cubic Fe2O3. In addition, the (100) lattice plane of NCDs also clearly appears in the SAED pattern, which is consistent with the HRTEM analysis. The above results verify the effective incorporation of NCDs with Fe2O3 nanosheets. The element mapping results are shown in Fig. 3g-3k, from which it can be visibly observed that the elements N, Fe, O, and P are evenly distributed on the nanosheet. This uniformly distributed and fixed structure will enable the material to fully contact with the electrolyte, which allows the substances between the interfaces to quickly and effectively diffuse electrolyte ions. In addition, the P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 display a typical type IV hysteresis loop (Fig. S2), indicating a mesoporous structure of the nanosheets. The specific surface area reaches 25.2 m2 g−1, and the main pore is located at 3.8 nm. It is known that the mesoporous structure can facilitate the rapid diffusion of the electrolyte and thus achieve enhanced electrochemical characteristics [38].

The XRD patterns of Co9S8/NCDs-2 and P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 nanoflowers are displayed in Fig. 4 a. The diffraction peaks in these two products match well with cubic Co9S8 (JCPDS 86–2273). Consistent with Fe2O3, the phosphating engineering does not change the crystal phase of Co9S8. The surface valence state analyses of Co9S8/NCDs-2 and P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 are shown in Fig. 4b-4f. Except for C, N, O, Co, and S elements, the additional P element is successfully introduced to P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 nanoflowers, as presented in Fig. 4b. As shown in Fig. S3, the N 1 s spectra can be fitted as four characteristic peaks, corresponding to pyridine N, pyrrole N, graphite N, and oxidized N, respectively. The Co 2p spectrum of Co9S8/NCDs-2 can be deconvoluted into six major peaks (Fig. 4c). The binding energies at 778.0 (793.1) eV and 780.4 (796.1) eV are derived from Co3+ and Co2+ species [39]. The other two peaks at 784.9 and 802.1 eV can be indexed as the satellite peaks of Co 2p. After phosphating, these characteristic peaks are positively shifted, indicating that the coordination environment of Co species changes due to the introduction of the phosphorus dopant [40]. In addition, the Co2+ species content in P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 significantly increases due to the partial reduction of Co3+ to Co2+ by reductive PH3 produced by thermal decomposition of NaH2PO2. In the S 2p spectrum of Co9S8/NCDs-2 (Fig. 4d), the peaks at 161.1 and 162.0 eV correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of S2- [41]. The characteristic S2 2- is located at 163.1 eV [42]. Similarly, the binding energies of S2- and S2 2- species in P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 are slightly larger than that in Co9S8/NCDs-2. In Fig. 4e, three peaks at 530.5, 531.3, and 532.5 eV are assigned to characteristic C = O, C-O, and O–H, respectively. By deconvolution, the O 1 s spectrum of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 reveals the presence of H2PO4 - and PO3 - species at 531.6 and 532.7 eV. The high-resolution P 2p spectrum (Fig. 4f) can be deconvolved into two characteristic peaks, attributing to P-Co (129.7 eV) and P-O (134.1 eV) bonds. The formation of the P-Co and P-O bonds further verifies the phosphorus doping and phosphate functionalization of Co9S8.

SEM and TEM were conducted to elucidate the morphology of bare Co9S8, Co9S8/NCDs-2, and P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 samples. The bare Co9S8 sample (Fig. 5 a) presents a typical flower-like hierarchical structure. As exhibited in Fig. 5b, the size of these nanoflowers becomes smaller after the combination with NCDs, mainly because the addition of NCDs inhibits the growth of Co9S8. After phosphate modification, the flower-like hierarchical structure of Co9S8 is maintained (Fig. 5c). However, these nanoflowers become thicker. From the high-magnification SEM image in Fig. 5d, it can be seen that the nanoflowers are constituted by interconnected two-dimensional thin sheets. These sheets are uniform and highly dense, so the flower-like hierarchical structure can maintain its integrity after ultrasonic treatment, representing the structural stability of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2. The TEM image exhibited in Fig. 5e further verifies the nanosheet stacked flower-like architecture. Moreover, the nanosheet surface is composed of a large number of nanoparticles (Fig. 5f). The HRTEM image in Fig. 5g demonstrates distinct lattice fringes with spacings of 0.29 and 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (311) and (100) crystal planes of Co9S8 and NCDs. In the SAED pattern, the concentric diffraction rings can be indexed to the (311) and (440) crystal planes of cubic Co9S8 and the (100) crystal plane of NCDs, demonstrating the excellent recombination of the nanoflowers. The distribution of elements in the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 flower-like structure was verified by element mapping characterization. As shown in Fig. 5h-5m, the N, Co, S, P, and O elements are evenly distributed in the composite. Moreover, based on the EDS spectrum (Fig. S4), the atomic ratio of Co/S is determined to be about 9:8, which proves that the crystalline phase of the substance belongs to Co9S8. The specific surface area of the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 flower-like structure is 7.8 m2 g−1, and the main pore is located at 1.6 nm.

The three-electrode tests were carried out in 3 M KOH to evaluate the energy storage properties of Fe2O3-based electrodes. Fig. S5a presents the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of bare Fe2O3, P-Fe2O3, and Fe2O3/NCDs nanosheets at 25 mV s−1. All the curves show irregular rectangular shapes, confirming the pseudocapacitive behavior of these Fe2O3-based electrodes. Impressively, the CV enclosing areas of P-Fe2O3 and Fe2O3/NCDs electrodes are more extensive than bare Fe2O3. The enhanced electrochemical features of Fe2O3 are attributed to the phosphate and NCDs modifications. No plateaus appear during the galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) tests (Fig. S5b), which is consistent with the CV results. The bare Fe2O3, P-Fe2O3, and Fe2O3/NCDs electrodes display different discharge time at 1 A g−1. Combined with the electrochemical capacitance calculation formula, the capacitances of bare Fe2O3, P-Fe2O3, and Fe2O3/NCDs modified with 10, 15, and 20 mg NCDs are 115, 244.8, 230.4, 267.8, and 177.6 F g−1, respectively. Excessive NCDs will hinder the contact between Fe2O3 and KOH electrolytes, resulting in the reduction of specific capacitance. Therefore, appropriate NCDs content is crucial to the regulation of electrochemical performance. Fig. 6 a shows the CV curves of P-Fe2O3/NCDs with different amounts of NaH2PO2·H2O. Clearly, the CV curve of the P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 electrode possesses the largest enclosing area. The specific capacity is 453 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 6b), which is better than P-Fe2O3/NCDs-1 (408.2 F g−1) and P-Fe2O3/NCDs-3 (299.2 F g−1). Moreover, the CV curves of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 exhibit a constant shape with increasing the scan rate (Fig. 6c), confirming its excellent reversibility. The hammer-shaped CV curves do not show prominent redox peaks, consistent with the GCD results illustrated in Fig. 6d. The specific capacitance of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 remains 248 F g−1 even at 20 A g−1, and the capacitance retention is 54.7 % compared with the initial value. As shown in Fig. 6e, it is worth noting that P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 delivers the highest specific capacitances at 1–20 A g−1 compared to the other Fe2O3-based electrodes. The rate capability of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 is also superior to other electrodes, such as Fe2O3/NCDs-2 (45.5 %) and bare Fe2O3 (38.3 %). Moreover, adjusting the mass ratio of Fe2O3/NCDs-2 and NaH2PO2·H2O can affect the electrochemical performance of the P-Fe2O3/NCDs electrode. When the mass ratio is 1:15, it is considered that an excessive PH3 atmosphere will cause the formation of more PO3 - species covered on the surface of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-3, resulting in significant degradation of the electrochemical property [43]. Long cycle life is a crucial feature for electrode materials. GCD measurement was conducted at a high current density of 15 A g−1 for 10,000 cycles to explore the cycling performance of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 (Fig. 6f). The capacitance retention of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 can maintain 91.0 % of the initial value with a coulombic efficiency of nearly 100 % after 10,000 cycles. The GCD profiles of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 for the 1st and 10000th cycles are illustrated in Fig. S5c, from which negligible change can be found, demonstrating the excellent cycling capability of the prepared P-Fe2O3/NCDs nanosheets. In the first 2000 cycles, the increasing trend of specific capacitance can be attributed to activating the passivate active material surface in alkaline electrolyte and the ion insertion and penetration inside the active material [44]. Besides, Table S1 compares the electrochemical features of previously reported iron-based electrodes with P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2. Impressively, the electrochemical properties of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 are comparable to the other electrode materials, revealing that the Fe2O3 nanosheets with phosphate and NCDs dual functionalization are beneficial to achieving enhanced properties.

The comprehensive electrochemical properties of the Co9S8-based electrodes were tested in 3 M KOH. Fig. S6a shows the CV curves of bare Co9S8, P-Co9S8, and Co9S8/NCDs nanoflowers at 25 mV s−1. The appearance peaks appear due to the reversible transformation between different cobalt oxidation states [45,46]. It is impressive that the CV-enclosed regions of P-Co9S8 and Co9S8/NCDs electrodes are wider than that of bare Co9S8. The enhancement of the electrochemical properties is attributed to the phosphate functionalization and effective modification of NCDs. There are apparent platforms in the GCD profiles at 2 A g−1 (Fig. S6b), which are consistent with the CV results. Obviously, the Co9S8/NCDs-2 delivers the optimized electrochemical capacities among a series of Co9S8-based electrodes. Fig. 7 a illustrates the CV curves of P-Co9S8/NCDs with different amounts of NaH2PO2·H2O. Clearly, the CV curve of the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 electrode possesses the largest enclosing area. The GCD profiles in Fig. 7b highlight the considerable difference in the electrochemical characteristics of Co9S8-based electrodes. It is evident that the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 electrode delivers the longest discharge time at 2 A g−1, further confirming that the multiple components functionalization plays a vital role in boosting the electrochemical properties of bare Co9S8. The CV curves of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 at 10 to 100 mV s−1 are revealed in Fig. 7c. The shape of the curves is retained with increasing the scan rate, indicating the high reversibility of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2. The Faradic behavior of P-Co9S8/NCDs can be verified by the nonlinear GCD profiles (Fig. 7d). The electrochemical capacity is calculated to be 569 C g−1 (1034.5 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 and retains 417 C g−1 (758.2 F g−1) at 15 A g−1. Moreover, the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 electrode also achieves the highest specific capacities at 1–15 A g−1 compared to the other electrodes (Fig. 7e). To further elucidate the electrochemical energy storage mechanism of the P-Co9S8/NCDs flower-like structure, the CV measurement was conducted at 0.2 to 1.0 mV s−1 (Fig. 7f). The diffusion and capacitive control processes were revealed according to the following relationships [3]:

wherein i, v, and i(V) represent the peak current, sweep rate, and the current at a fixed voltage. The value of variable b can be calculated from the slope of log (i) and log (v), as exhibited in Fig. 7g. If the b value is 0.5, the energy storage is controlled by the diffusion process, and if the b value is 1, the energy storage can be classified into capacitive characteristics [47]. The b values are 0.78 and 0.86 by fitting the peak currents at 0.2 to 1.0 mV s−1, indicating that the electrochemical behavior of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 involves diffusion (k2v1/2 ) and capacitive control (k1v) processes. The value of variable k1 can be calculated from the slope of i(V)/v1/2 and v1/2 . It can be seen from Fig. 7h that the capacitance contribution ratio of the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 electrode is 83.9 % at 0.2 mV s−1 and increases to 95.2 % at 1.0 mV s−1. Briefly, the dominant capacitive characteristics ensure P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 with rapid capacitance response and superior rate capability [48]. The GCD measurement was also conducted at 15 A g−1 to evaluate the cycle stability of the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 electrode. As illustrated in Fig. 7i and Fig S6c, the capacity retention increases to 110.9 % of the initial value after 10,000 cycles. The upward trend of specific capacity of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 can be attributed to the increase of active sites after activation of the flower-like hierarchical structure and is reasonable for cobalt-based electrode materials [49]. Most impressively, the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 electrode owns decent electrochemical properties comparable to previous reported cobalt-based electrodes for supercapacitors (Table S2).

In order to further understand the charge transport features of the Fe2O3 and Co9S8-based electrodes, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was studied in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz (Fig. S7). The equivalent series resistance (R s) represents the resistance associated with the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte and the electronic conductivity of the electrode and current collector. The conductivity of the electrode material increases as the Rs value decreases [50]. Herein, the Rs value increases in the following order: P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 (0.54 Ω) < Fe2O3/NCDs-2 (0.69 Ω) < bare Fe2O3 (0.73 Ω), reflecting the lowest intrinsic conductivity of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2. The diameter of the semicircle in the high-frequency region is related to the faradic reaction's charge transfer resistance (Rct ). The Rct value of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 is 0.39 Ω, which is smaller than Fe2O3/NCDs-2 (0.55 Ω) and bare Fe2O3 (0.65 Ω). This indicates the faster charge-transfer process for the P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 electrode. The same phenomenon also occurs in P-Co9S8/NCDs-2, which possesses lower R s and R ct values of 0.45 and 0.32 Ω, respectively. The slope of the curve in the low-frequency region is controlled by the Warburg impedance (W o) arising from the ion diffusion [51]. Apparently, the low-frequency curves of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 and P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 electrodes are closer to the vertical, suggesting the fast ion diffusion characteristics of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 and P-Co9S8/NCDs-2. In addition, negligible changes are observed in the Nyquist plots of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 and P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 even after 10,000 cycles, revealing the ultra-long stability of the electrode materials. Therefore, the optimized electrochemical characteristics of P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 and P-Co9S8/NCDs-2 can be attributed to the synergy interaction of NCDs and phosphate, which tune the electrical conductivity and electronic structure of Co9S8, thus significantly promoting the charge transfer and boosting the reaction kinetics [52].

The P-Co9S8/NCDs-2//P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 hybrid supercapacitor device was assembled to evaluate the energy storage characteristics of dual functionalized Fe2O3 nanosheets and Co9S8 nanoflowers more comprehensively. Fig. 8 a displays the CV curves for P-Co9S8/NCDs-2//P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 hybrid supercapacitor at 10–100 mV s−1. With the increase in scan rate, the overall curves of the CV do not change significantly, confirming its high reversible performance. The GCD profiles of the device under different voltage ranges are revealed in Fig. 8b. A prominent charging platform appears under the voltage window of 1.55 V, indicating that the energy storage behavior is insufficient to reach 1.55 V. Therefore, the optimized potential range is positioned at 0–1.5 V. The profiles display good symmetry in Fig. 8c, demonstrating the excellent electrochemical features of the P-Co9S8/NCDs-2//P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 hybrid supercapacitor. Under the charging/discharging platform of 1.5 V, the specific capacitances of the device achieve 217.9, 194.7, 164.7, 142.7, 130, and 118.7 F g−1 at 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, and 20 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 8d). The energy density of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2//P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 can reach 85.1 Wh kg−1 at 937.5 W kg−1 and remains 46.4 Wh kg−1 at a high power density of 18750 W kg−1, as illustrated in Fig. 8e. The cycle stability is the prerequisite to judging whether the device is feasible. The 20,000 cycles of GCD tests were implemented in 3 M KOH (Fig. 8f). The cycle stability measurement only causes 6.1 % of the capacitance attenuation. In addition, the coulombic efficiency of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2//P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 is nearly 100 %, and the GCD profile after 20,000 cycles is negligible unchanged (Fig. S8). These results highlight the superior durability of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2//P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2. As listed in Table S3, the excellent electrochemical features of P-Co9S8/NCDs-2//P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 also have evident advantages compared with the published devices, such as a-Ni-Co-S/c-MnS/rGO//Fe2O3 NDs/rGO, Ni3Mn1Co@Co9S8-QDs/NF//Fe2O3@C/CC, Sn-Co3O4//Fe2O3@GF, MxCo3-xS4@NiMoS4//Fe2O3/NG, and NiCoCO3(OH)2//Fe2O3/rGO. To further verify the practical applicability of the device, two electrolytic cells were connected with a 2.4 V LED lamp to simulate the operation of electronic equipment. As shown in Fig. 8g-8i, the LED lamp remains brightness after 5 min, demonstrating the feasibility of our assembled P-Co9S8/NCDs-2//P-Fe2O3/NCDs-2 hybrid supercapacitor for renewable energy applications.

In summary, Fe2O3 nanosheets and Co9S8 nanoflowers with phosphate and NCDs dual functionalization have been designed by a systematic strategy. The phosphate and NCDs modifications can significantly optimize the electrical conductivity and electronic structure of Fe2O3 and Co9S8, thus remarkably promoting their electrochemical characteristics. The optimized P-Fe2O3/NCDs and P-Co9S8/NCDs electrodes achieve superior electrochemical features with enhanced specific capacitance/capacity and satisfying cycling stabilities after 10,000 cycles. Moreover, the hybrid supercapacitor comprising P-Fe2O3/NCDs anode and P-Co9S8/NCDs cathode delivers a maximum energy density of 85.1 Wh kg−1 and ultra-high stability of 93.9 % capacitance retention over 20,000 cycles. This advanced study provides new opportunities to construct novel electrode materials for hybrid supercapacitor applications.
